caveats: a: The occupation source covers the broader three-digit NAICS 459000 group and includes unrelated specialty retailers.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and exclude self-employed owners.
a: Existing POS, e-commerce, pattern software, and machine automation are not separately measured.
rho: BLS projections cover all industries and a ten-year horizon, not this industry and five years.
rho: Cricut is a scaled manufacturer/platform rather than an LMM retailer.
rho: Self-checkout, e-commerce, and machine automation are not identical to AI implementation.
e: Customer preference for education does not establish recurring service revenue at the firm level.
e: Sewing-machine-only retailers and reupholstery services are classified elsewhere, creating boundary leakage.
e: The frozen n input is missing, so eligible firm count cannot be derived in this packet.
s5: The quilting survey describes customers, not business owners, and has a long, opt-in questionnaire with likely selection bias.
s5: The Census age statistic is cross-industry and based on data year 2018 responding owners.
s5: BizBuySell skews toward transactions below the target LMM EBITDA band.
q: The consumer survey sample is highly engaged and likely overrepresents local-shop users.
q: Repair, instruction, long-arm, subscription, and merchandise pricing have different pass-through dynamics.
q: Cricut's economics reflect a proprietary hardware-software ecosystem unavailable to most retailers.
d5: Participation and spending intentions are not direct service-quantity measures.
d5: Survey respondents are self-selected from major quilting brand lists and may overstate engagement.
d5: Cricut users span paper, vinyl, gifts, and other crafts beyond sewing and needlework.
o: The quilting survey may overrepresent highly engaged local-shop customers.
o: Digital substitution varies between fabric selection, yarn, machine repair, long-arm work, patterns, and instruction.
o: The estimate applies only to the narrowed service-bearing basket, not ordinary merchandise demand.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Sewing, Needlework, and Piece Goods Retailers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat consumer, guild, school, and institutional relationships combining merchandise margin with repair or service fees, classes, clubs, subscriptions, project programs, machine support, or long-arm services; recurring programs often stimulate repeat materials purchases.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is primarily product retail but contains a distinct service-bearing subset of machine-support, repair, long-arm, instruction, club, subscription, and repeat-program operators. The lens is narrowed to that subset for business-model coherence because ordinary piece-goods sales are transactional.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure fabric, yarn, notion, pattern, and online merchandise sellers; machine-only appliance retailers; reupholstery firms; independent makers selling finished goods; captive operations; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and firms without a recurring or repeat outsourced-service relationship.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market sewing, quilting, yarn, needlework, and piece-goods retailers with repeat external-customer services such as sewing-machine service coordination, repair, long-arm quilting, paid classes, clubs, block-of-the-month programs, subscriptions, or recurring institutional supply and instruction.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader NAICS 459000 parent, not 459130, and does not measure task exposure. The estimate maps high AI exposure to product and pattern search, customer messaging, course promotion, scheduling, order entry, purchasing, bookkeeping, and content creation; partial exposure to sales and instruction; and low exposure to fabric handling, machine repair, long-arm work, cutting, stocking, and hands-on teaching. Parent-industry mixing can bias the inferred occupation mix in either direction.
caveats: The occupation source covers the broader three-digit NAICS 459000 group and includes unrelated specialty retailers.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and exclude self-employed owners.
Existing POS, e-commerce, pattern software, and machine automation are not separately measured.
a.change_evidence: A wage- and task-level time study across independent quilt shops, yarn shops, sewing-machine dealers, and long-arm operators, including actual AI pilots, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The parent retail group has 56.80% of employment in sales occupations, including 37.94% retail salespersons and 9.17% cashiers, plus 9.99% in office and administrative support. AI can streamline substantial information and coordination work, but tactile product advice, physical service, repair, and live instruction remain central in the narrowed lens.

d5.bridge: The Quilter's Survey measures participant counts and reported time and spending, not constant-price quantity for the narrowed service basket. Cricut measures an adjacent digital-crafting ecosystem. The estimate uses a decade-long stable base of 9–11 million active quilters and 80% reporting the same or greater time and dollars, while discounting for a post-pandemic decline in active sewists, inflation-driven stash use, and platform revenue declines. Service demand may be steadier than machine sales but is exposed to aging participation and discretionary spending.
caveats: Participation and spending intentions are not direct service-quantity measures.
Survey respondents are self-selected from major quilting brand lists and may overstate engagement.
Cricut users span paper, vinyl, gifts, and other crafts beyond sewing and needlework.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year transaction counts for classes, repair, long-arm services, subscriptions, block programs, and recurring institutional orders from shop POS and service-ticket systems would materially move the estimate.
d5.rationale: The 2024 survey reports stable quilting participation for a decade, strong weekly engagement, and sustained time and spending, supporting a flat real-demand base. However, active sewists declined from pandemic levels, respondents report using existing fabric stashes due to inflation, and Cricut revenue fell in 2023, 2024, and 2025. These offsetting signals support a near-flat five-year ratio with a meaningful downside and modest upside.

e.bridge: The 2024 Quilter's Survey measures customer preferences, not eligible firms, while parent-industry OEWS measures workers. The estimate uses the importance of hands-on classes to local-shop selection, persistent local-shop preference, and the presence of self-enrichment teachers and repair workers in the broader occupation mix as evidence for a service-bearing subset. It then discounts because many shops offer occasional free help rather than recurring paid outsourced services and because parent-industry repair employment is heavily influenced by other retail categories.
caveats: Customer preference for education does not establish recurring service revenue at the firm level.
Sewing-machine-only retailers and reupholstery services are classified elsewhere, creating boundary leakage.
The frozen n input is missing, so eligible firm count cannot be derived in this packet.
e.change_evidence: A stratified census of industry merchants measuring paid class, repair, long-arm, subscription, club, and institutional-program revenue alongside EBITDA would directly estimate eligible share.
e.rationale: Quilt and sewing shops often differentiate through classes, clubs, project programs, machine support, long-arm services, and repair coordination, creating more service adjacency than ordinary specialty retail. Yet the NAICS definition centers sales of supplies, fabric, yarn, patterns, and machines, so many firms remain purely transactional and ineligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: Survey channel preferences, BLS occupation projections, and Cricut's digital workflow reveal substitution paths but do not measure the year-five operator-required share for qualifying firms. The estimate allows online retail, free video instruction, AI design, guided software, self-service clubs, and manufacturer ecosystems to remove portions of demand, while retaining local operator need for tactile assortment, repair, machine support, hands-on instruction, long-arm work, social accountability, and exceptions.
caveats: The quilting survey may overrepresent highly engaged local-shop customers.
Digital substitution varies between fabric selection, yarn, machine repair, long-arm work, patterns, and instruction.
The estimate applies only to the narrowed service-bearing basket, not ordinary merchandise demand.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal channel migration and customer-cohort data for repair, classes, long-arm services, subscriptions, clubs, and project programs, including customer adoption of video and AI self-service alternatives, would materially refine the estimate.
o.rationale: Local shops retain experiential relevance: 48% of surveyed quilters most often bought fabric locally, and hands-on education was a shop-selection factor. Yet 31.8% reported searching and shopping more online in 2024, free YouTube video was a primary education source, and AI-enabled design platforms can guide projects directly. The durable operator share is therefore material but not complete.

q.bridge: Consumer survey and adjacent-platform evidence describe shopping preferences, digitization, and pricing pressure rather than retained AI benefit. The estimate applies stronger retention to locally differentiated repair, hands-on instruction, long-arm work, and curated project programs, and lower retention to comparable merchandise, online classes, subscriptions, and routine administration. Local preference may overstate pricing power because survey respondents are highly engaged quilters.
caveats: The consumer survey sample is highly engaged and likely overrepresents local-shop users.
Repair, instruction, long-arm, subscription, and merchandise pricing have different pass-through dynamics.
Cricut's economics reflect a proprietary hardware-software ecosystem unavailable to most retailers.
q.change_evidence: Actual service-contract, class, repair-ticket, subscription-renewal, and project-program price histories before and after automation, paired with local competitive bids and gross-margin bridges, would identify pass-through.
q.rationale: Savings from administrative AI are not automatically passed through under posted service and retail prices. Local differentiation provides some protection: 61% of surveyed quilters preferred local quilt shops, compared with 16% preferring online, and hands-on education influenced shop choice. Still, online search and shopping, free video education, national platforms, and price competition should share a meaningful portion over time.

rho.bridge: BLS national projections and Cricut's AI-enabled workflow show technical and market direction rather than five-year implementation among qualifying LMM retailers. The estimate translates declining cashier and customer-service employment plus available AI design and self-service tools into an implementation share, then discounts for fragmented legacy systems, small management teams, data quality, customer demographics, safety, and the need for human instruction and repair judgment.
caveats: BLS projections cover all industries and a ten-year horizon, not this industry and five years.
Cricut is a scaled manufacturer/platform rather than an LMM retailer.
Self-checkout, e-commerce, and machine automation are not identical to AI implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year deployment and verified labor-hour data from craft POS, class-management, subscription, service-ticket, and machine-dealer software vendors would materially refine implementation.
rho.rationale: BLS projects cashier employment down 10% and customer-service representatives down 5% from 2024 to 2034. Cricut already offers AI-generated cut-ready images and guided flows, demonstrating practical automation adjacent to sewing and crafts. Independent operators can adopt commodity tools for marketing and administration, but process integration and customer change will be uneven.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age data and BizBuySell retail transactions cover broad employer and retail populations, not qualifying sewing and needlework firms in the target EBITDA band. The estimate converts succession pressure and observable retail liquidity into a five-year control-transfer probability, reducing for closures, internal transfers, Main Street deals below the EBITDA band, and owner-dependent expertise, but allowing modestly higher succession pressure given the older core quilting customer ecosystem.
caveats: The quilting survey describes customers, not business owners, and has a long, opt-in questionnaire with likely selection bias.
The Census age statistic is cross-industry and based on data year 2018 responding owners.
BizBuySell skews toward transactions below the target LMM EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal and mandate data from sewing-machine dealer networks, quilt and yarn shop brokers, DealStats, Axial, GF Data, and SBA lenders, matched to owner age, EBITDA, and service mix, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Census found 51% of responding employer-business owners age 55 or older, and the 2024 quilting survey describes the average respondent as retired and in her 60s, suggesting an aging ecosystem even though customers are not owners. An active independent-retail sale market exists, but its typical transaction is far smaller than the target band and transferable service systems may be limited.

principal_driver: Tactile product selection, physical machine and quilting services, and hands-on community education preserve a local operator alongside AI-assisted administration.
principal_weakness: The eligible service-bearing firm share and target-band transfer market are not directly measured, and demand faces digital self-service plus demographic aging.
